HRV: what heart rate variability actually measures

A healthy heart does not tick like a metronome. Between beats there are sometimes 850 milliseconds, sometimes 910, sometimes 780. This unevenness is called heart rate variability (HRV) — and, counterintuitively, the higher it is, the better.

The metric shown by every fitness ring and band is one of the few honest numbers in biohacking. We explain what heart rate variability is, why "higher is better," and how to read it without fooling yourself.
A healthy heart does not tick like a metronome. Between beats there are sometimes 850 milliseconds, sometimes 910, sometimes 780. This unevenness is called
(HRV) — and, counterintuitively, the higher it is, the better.
A window into the nervous system
The heart's rhythm is governed by the autonomic nervous system — the part that works without conscious involvement. It has two arms: the
("fight or flight" — speeds up) and the
("rest and recover" — slows down). They pull on the rhythm simultaneously, and the constant struggle of the two regulators creates that very unevenness.

Hence the logic of the metric. High variability means the parasympathetic system is active: the body is in recovery mode, resources are available. Low variability means the system is locked in sympathetic mode: stress, lack of sleep, illness, overtraining, alcohol the night before. In this state the heart beats more evenly — and that is a bad sign, not a good one.
HRV does not measure "health" and does not measure "stress" directly. It shows how much flexibility the nervous system has left — the ability to switch between tension and recovery.
Why comparing yourself to others is pointless

Absolute HRV numbers depend on age, genetics, sex, and measurement method so strongly that "norms" make almost no sense: one healthy person has a morning reading of 30 ms, another 90 ms, and both are fine. The only meaningful comparison is
you today versus your own average
Look at the trend over 7–14 days, not yesterday's number: from day to day the metric is noisy.
A sharp drop relative to your personal baseline is a reason for an easy day: it is an early signal of overload or an oncoming illness, often ahead of symptoms.
You cannot compare readings from different devices: they count differently, at different times of day, with different sensors.
The best measurement conditions are night or morning right after waking, lying down, at the same time.

The list suspiciously overlaps with the boring fundamentals of health — and that is a good sign of an honest metric: regular sleep at the same time, aerobic exercise (with recovery afterward), avoiding alcohol in the evening, breathing practices with a slow exhale — 5–6 breaths per minute noticeably activate the parasympathetic system right during the exercise. As a curiosity: the effect of "box breathing" and similar techniques on HRV is one of the most reproducible results in this field.
If the ring shows a number every morning, let it be not a cause for anxiety but what it should be: feedback. A metric is good exactly to the extent that the actions it suggests are boring.
The material is educational in nature and does not replace consultation with a doctor.
